skill.name: pydantic
skill.description: Data validation and settings management using Python type annotations with Pydantic v2
skill.summary: Data validation and settings management using Python type annotations with Pydantic v2
skill.author: bossjones

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown backticks, frontmatter text, and inline examples as command execution, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance. Review found these are documentation examples for Pydantic v2, with no executable scripts, prompt injection, network access, or data exfiltration. The only retained risk factor is low-severity external command guidance for installing dependencies with uv.

low_findings.title: Documentation-Only Dependency Installation Commands
low_findings.description: The skill lists uv add commands for installing Pydantic and optional extras. These are normal setup instructions and are not executed by the skill, but users should review packages before installing community dependencies.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are under the Requirements section and are presented as inline documentation. They are external commands, but there is no script or automation that executes them.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives from Markdown Formatting
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick findings correspond to Markdown code fences and inline code examples. The high weak cryptography and low reconnaissance detections map to descriptive text or validation examples, not executable behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows Pydantic documentation examples, model definitions, validators, and serialization examples. No executable wrapper, shell interpolation, weak cryptographic implementation, or system reconnaissance code was found.

content.value_statement: Python applications often need reliable validation, conversion, and serialization for external data. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through practical Pydantic v2 patterns.
actual_capabilities: Defines Pydantic v2 models with typed fields, defaults, aliases, and constraints.
Shows validation from dictionaries, JSON strings, and standalone TypeAdapter use.
Explains strict model configuration, assignment validation, extra-field handling, and enum serialization.
Demonstrates field validators, model validators, and validation error handling.
Covers serialization controls, secret fields, excluded fields, and JSON-compatible output.
Includes nested model patterns for structured user, address, task, and product data.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not include runnable helper scripts or tests.
Examples are compact and may need adaptation for large production schemas.
It focuses on Pydantic v2 and does not cover migration from Pydantic v1 in depth.
It does not replace project-specific security review for validation boundaries.

use_cases.title: Validate API Payloads
use_cases.description: Create request and response models that reject malformed data before it reaches application logic.

use_cases.title: Model Application Settings
use_cases.description: Design typed configuration objects with clear defaults, strict fields, and predictable serialization.

use_cases.title: Standardize Data Contracts
use_cases.description: Translate loose dictionaries or JSON data into documented Python models for shared team workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Pydantic v2 model for this data shape. Include field types, sensible defaults, and a short validation example.

prompt_templates.title: Add Field Constraints
prompt_templates.prompt: Update this Pydantic model with Field constraints, aliases, and default factories. Explain each constraint in simple terms.

prompt_templates.title: Design Nested Validation
prompt_templates.prompt: Design nested Pydantic v2 models for this domain object. Include custom validators and clear validation error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Production Schema
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Pydantic v2 schema for strictness, serialization risks, secret handling, and maintainability. Recommend targeted changes.

output_examples.input: A developer asks for a user profile model with email, signup time, and active status.
output_examples.output: The assistant returns a concise model design, explains automatic type conversion, and notes where stricter validation should be enabled.

output_examples.input: A team wants to prevent passwords and internal fields from appearing in serialized responses.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends secret field handling, explicit field exclusion, and separate public response models.

output_examples.input: A data pipeline receives inconsistent lists of numbers and needs predictable validation.
output_examples.output: The assistant suggests TypeAdapter-based validation and describes how invalid items should be reported.

best_practices: Use strict configuration for trust boundaries where automatic type coercion could hide bad input.
Keep validation rules close to the fields and use model validators for cross-field checks.
Separate internal models from serialized response models when secrets or private fields exist.
anti_patterns: Do not rely on broad primitive types when field constraints can express expected values.
Do not serialize full models by default when they contain passwords, tokens, or internal fields.
Do not mix Pydantic v1 and v2 APIs without an explicit migration plan.

faq.question: Does this skill execute code?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown guide with examples and setup commands for users to run manually.

faq.question: Which Pydantic version does it cover?
faq.answer: It focuses on Pydantic v2 patterns, including BaseModel, ConfigDict, validators, serializers, and TypeAdapter.

faq.question: Can it help with API request validation?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides patterns for typed request models, field constraints, nested data, and validation error handling.

faq.question: Does it cover serialization safety?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows excluded fields, secret strings, include and exclude options, and JSON-compatible dumps.

faq.question: Is it useful for settings management?
faq.answer: Partly. It explains model configuration patterns, but it does not deeply cover pydantic-settings.

faq.question: What should users review before using it?
faq.answer: Users should confirm package installation commands, adapt examples to project rules, and test validation behavior.
